coredata.dc:title: Pentaethylenehexamine and histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots for ultrasensitive fluorescence detection of microRNA with target and molecular beacon double cycle amplification strategy
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Nana
Li, Ruiyi
Li, Zaijun
Yang, Yongqiang
Wang, Guangli
Gu, Zhiguo
coredata.dc:description: The paper reports the synthesis of pentaethylenehexamine and histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (PEHA-GQD-His). The as-synthesized PEHA-GQD-His was designed as the fluorescence probe for microRNA fluorescence biosensing nanoplatform coupled with molecular beacon double cycle amplification strategy. In the strategy the target microRNA specifically binds the molecular beacon to trigger the target cycle and the molecular beacon cycle, leading to an efficient DNA nanoassembly process on the PEHA-GQD-His surface. The resulted G-quadruplexes properly fold and bind hemin to produce stable G-quadruplex/hemin complexes. Then, the fluorescence of PEHA-GQD-His is significantly quenched via the photoinduced electron transfer by the hemin attached on the PEHA-GQD-His and the electron acceptor of O2, which is in situ generated by the decomposition of H2O2 under the catalysis of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzymes. The study indicates that the design of target and beacon double cycle creates highly specific and efficient DNA nanoassembly on the PEHA-GQD-His surface. The DNAzyme-modulated double quenching mechanism achieves to high fluorescence quenching efficiency of PEHA-GQD-His. The introduction of pentaethylenehexamine increases the fluorescence emission intensity of PEHA-GQD-His. The introduction of histidine in PEHA-GQD-His remarkably enhances the catalytic activity of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzymes towards H2O2. As a result, the proposed biosensing nanoplatform exhibits an ultrasensitive fluorescence response towards microRNA. Its fluorescence linearly reduces with increasing microRNA-141 concentration in the range of 1.0 × 10−18-1.0 × 10-12 M with the detection limit of 4.3 × 10-19 M. The analytical method has been successfully applied to fluorescence detection of microRNA-141 in human serum.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
DNA nanoassembly
DNAzyme
Fluorescence quenching
MicroRNA

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Pentaethylenehexamine and histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dot (PEHA-GQD-His) preparation
Conjugation of linker DNA (LDNA) with PEHA-GQD-His
Fluorescence assay for detection of miRNA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (A) (Insert: particle size distribution), HRTEM image (B) (Insert: FFTP image), AFM image (C) and FTIR pattern (D) of the PEHA-GQD-His.
Fluorescence excitation (a) and emission spectra (b) of the PEHA-GQD-His. Inset: Optical photographs of the PEHA-GQD-His under the white light (c) and ultraviolet light irradiation (d).
Principle of the miRNA fluorescence biosensing nanoplatform.

A: Fluorescence spectra of the mixture of LDNA-capped PEHA-GQD-His, hemin and H2O2 (a), the mixture of LDNA-capped PEHA-GQD-His, 1.0 × 10−12 M miRNA-141, H1, H2, hemin and H2O2 (b), the mixture of LDNA-capped PEHA-GQD-His, MB, 1.0 × 10−12 M miRNA-141, H1, hemin and H2O2 (c), and the mixture of LDNA-capped PEHA-GQD-His, MB, 1.0 × 10−12 M miRNA-141, H1, H2, hemin and H2O2 (d). B: The ΔF value for no cycle, single cycle and double cycle.

A: Fluorescence spectra of the mixture of LDNA-capped PEHA-GQD-His, 1.0 × 10−12 M miRNA-141, MB, H1 and H2 (a), the mixture of LDNA-capped PEHA-GQD-His, 1.0 × 10−12 M miRNA-141, MB, H1, H2 and hemin (b), and the mixture of LDNA-capped PEHA-GQD-His, 1.0 × 10−12 M miRNA-141, MB, H1 and H2, hemin and H2O2 (c). B: The relationship of ΔF with the mean of fluorescence quenching.
Fluorescence responses caused by 1.0 × 10−12 M of miRNA-141, 1.0 × 10-10 M of miRNA21, miRNA-429, miRNA-155, M-1 and M-2.

Strand displacement reaction and Mg2+-dependent
DNAzyme catalysis reaction

Pentaethylenehexamine and histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The results for miRNA-141 detection in human serum samples (N = 5).

head.ce:title: Pentaethylenehexamine and histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots for ultrasensitive fluorescence detection of microRNA with target and molecular beacon double cycle amplification strategy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids
School of Chemical and Material Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu graphene inspection technology key laboratory, Jiangsu Province Special Equipment Safety Supervision and Inspection Institute Branch of Wuxi, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu graphene inspection technology key laboratory
Jiangsu Province Special Equipment Safety Supervision and Inspection Institute Branch of Wuxi

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The study designed and synthesized pentaethylenehexamine and histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dot (PEHA-GQD-His). The PEHA-GQD-His was used for fabrication of microRNA fluorescence biosensing based on target and molecular beacon double cycle amplification strategy.

The paper reports the synthesis of pentaethylenehexamine and histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (PEHA-GQD-His). The as-synthesized PEHA-GQD-His was designed as the fluorescence probe for microRNA fluorescence biosensing nanoplatform coupled with molecular beacon double cycle amplification strategy. In the strategy the target microRNA specifically binds the molecular beacon to trigger the target cycle and the molecular beacon cycle, leading to an efficient DNA nanoassembly process on the PEHA-GQD-His surface. The resulted G-quadruplexes properly fold and bind hemin to produce stable G-quadruplex/hemin complexes. Then, the fluorescence of PEHA-GQD-His is significantly quenched via the photoinduced electron transfer by the hemin attached on the PEHA-GQD-His and the electron acceptor of O2, which is in situ generated by the decomposition of H2O2 under the catalysis of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzymes. The study indicates that the design of target and beacon double cycle creates highly specific and efficient DNA nanoassembly on the PEHA-GQD-His surface. The DNAzyme-modulated double quenching mechanism achieves to high fluorescence quenching efficiency of PEHA-GQD-His. The introduction of pentaethylenehexamine increases the fluorescence emission intensity of PEHA-GQD-His. The introduction of histidine in PEHA-GQD-His remarkably enhances the catalytic activity of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzymes towards H2O2. As a result, the proposed biosensing nanoplatform exhibits an ultrasensitive fluorescence response towards microRNA. Its fluorescence linearly reduces with increasing microRNA-141 concentration in the range of 1.0 × 10−18-1.0 × 10-12 M with the detection limit of 4.3 × 10-19 M. The analytical method has been successfully applied to fluorescence detection of microRNA-141 in human serum.

ce:section.ce:para: MicroRNA (miRNA) is non-protein-coding single-stranded RNA that consists of only 18–24 bases [1]. The studies have demonstrated that miRNAs play key regulatory roles in gene expression, cell proliferation, apoptosis, viral defense, metabolism, hematopoietic differentiation and tumorigenesis [2]. Recently, tumor-derived cell-free miRNAs received much attention as the biomarker of cancers [3]. These miRNAs are appear in various body fluids such as urine, saliva and plasma, and hold own valuable characters including high stability, presence of tissue-specific sources, profusion, resistance to degrading RNase enzymes and non-invasiveness [4–7]. The miRNAs impact cancer at different levels, thus their quantitative measurements are critical to early diagnosis and diseases cure [8]. However, some features of miRNAs, including small size, high sequence similarity and low content, emerge as the obstacles of proper detection [9–12].

Many analytical techniques have been well established for detection of miRNAs such as northern blotting [13], oligonucleotide microarray analysis [14], electrochemical methods [15] and fluorescent methods [16]. Northern blotting is mostly used method for analysis of miRNAs expression [17]. But, northern blotting is difficult to be applied in diagnostic practice, because it lacks sufficient sensitivity for direct detection of miRNAs in real clinical samples [18]. Oligonucleotide microarray analysis achieves to high-throughput detection of target miRNAs, but its ideal efficiency does not overcome its limitations. The results obtained by the microarray analysis often needs be further verified by other techniques [19]. Electrochemical method is of high sensitivity and selectivity, but its instability is inherent weakness [20]. Fluorescence assay attracted more attention for detection of miRNAs due to its simple operation and high sensitivity [21–23]. To improve the sensitivity, various signal amplification strategies have been well established and applied in fluorescent miRNAs sensing in the recent years [24]. Although enzyme-based amplification strategies have been widely used for miRNAs analysis at a very low level, but the use of enzyme often requires strict control conditions due to the instability of enzyme [25]. To overcome the above problems, the non-enzymatic signal amplification approaches have been investigated to fluorescence detection of miRNAs [26]. In the non-enzymatic amplification strategies, the toehold-mediated strand displacement reaction become one of the most powerful tools for receiving and transferring input [27,28]. In such a reaction initiator, the miRNA specifically binds harpin DNA probe to produce overhanging single-stranded domain, causing a branch migration and strand displacement process. By special target-triggered cycle, one continuous strand displacement can be achieved and leads to hundred-fold signal amplification [29]. Despite great progress has made, the development of more reliable analytical techniques for fluorescence detection of miRNAs is highly anticipated.

Graphene quantum dot (GQD) is a zero-dimensional graphitic nanocrystal with the size of only several nanometers [30]. Excellent photochemical and physicochemical stability, ease of functionalization, water solubility and biocompatibility make GQD become an ideal fluorescence probe for optical detection [31] and bioimaging [32,33]. Recently, the application of GQDs in fluorescence detection of DNA based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) has aroused great interest [34–36]. Due to large excitation range and tunable emission, GQD allows simple selection of excitation wavelength that corresponds with the absorption spectrum of acceptor molecule while avoiding its direct excitation. This greatly limits the background interference of fluorescence sensing assays and therefore supports the use of GQDs as a FRET donor. In addition, large electron delocalization of GQDs facilitates physical adsorption of DNA on the graphene sheets via non-covalent π-π stacking interactions and hydrophobic interactions with DNA nucleobases. These interactions bring highly sensitive fluorescence response of GQDs towards DNA [37]. However, the exploration of GQDs as a potential FRET nanosensor in DNA detection, in particular as the FRET nanoquencher, still remains at a preliminary stage.

Herein, we designed and synthesized a pentaethylenehexamine and histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dot (termed as PEHA-GQD-His) for ultrasensitive fluorescence detection of miRNA with target and molecular beacon double cycle amplification strategy. The study shows that the unique structure makes PEHA-GQD-His offers strong fluorescence emission and good ability to improve the catalytic activity of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzymes towards H2O2. The DNA assembly on the PEHA-GQD-His surface coupled with DNAzyme-modulated double quenching create a significant signal amplification for miRNA fluorescence biosensing. The as-proposed biosensor provides much better sensitivity, selectivity and reproducibility compared with the present biosensors for miRNAs.

Citric acid, pentaethylenehexamine, histidine, hemin, N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), diethyl carbonate (DECP) and ethyl-3-(3′-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide (EDC) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Mainland, China). All oligonucleotides are synthesized and purified by Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. and their sequences are listed in Table 1 . The DNA stock solutions are prepared in Tris/Mg/K buffer containing 10 mM Tris-HCl, 4 mM MgCl2 and 15 mM KCl at pH 8.0, and stored at −20 °C. The oligonucleotides are determined by measuring the UV–vis absorption at 260 nm. Before use, MB, H1 and H2 sequence were heated at 95 °C for 10 min and slowly cooled to room temperature at 1 °C min−1 to form stable hairpin structure. Other reagents used were purchased from Shanghai Chemical Company (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water treated by DECP was used throughout the experiment. To treat ultrapure water, 1.0 ml of DECP was added into 1000 ml of ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) prepared using Milli-Q purification system. Followed placing overnight and heating at 121℃ for 20 min.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) image was conducted on a JEOL 2010 FEG microscope at 200 keV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was carried out on a PHI 5700 ESCA spectrometer. Atomic force microscopic (AFM) image was obtained on a Multimode 8 force microscope (Bruker, Germany). Infrared spectra (IR) were recorded on a Nicolet FT-IR 6700 spectrometer. Fluorescence spectrum and intensity were recorded on a Cary Esclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent, Japan). UV–vis spectra were recorded on a TU-1901 spectrometer with a DH-2000 deuterium and tungsten halogen light source in absorbance mode (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd., China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pentaethylenehexamine and histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dot (PEHA-GQD-His) preparation
ce:section.ce:para: PEHA-GQD-His was prepared by using a reported method [30]. In a typical synthesis, citric acid (0.02 mol), pentaethylenehexamine (0.002 mol) and histidine (0.02 mol) were dissolved in ultra pure water with stirring and subsequently evaporated at 80 °C to remove all water. After that, the mixture was heated at 180 °C for 3 h. The resulted PEHA-GQD-His was dissolved in ultra pure water and then neutralized to pH 7 with the NaOH solution to form a transparent PEHA-GQD-His solution (40 mg ml−1). The solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag with a 3 kDa cut-off molecular weight with change of water every 6 h before the precipitate occurred in the bag. The solution in bag was collected and then dried by free-drying to obtain PEHA-GQD-His product.

Quantum yield measurement was carried out by dissolving quinine sulphate in o.1 M H2SO4 (used as reference). The PEHA-GQD-His dispersion was used as such. The absorbance of respective sample was measured on the spectrophotometer. Quantum yield was calculated from following Eq. (1) [38]:
(1)
Q =
Q R
m s
m r
n s 2
n r 2
where, Q presents the quantum yield of PEHA-GQD-His, QR is the quantum yield of quinine sulphate, ms is slope of the plot integrated fluorescence intensity vs. the absorbance of PEHA-GQD-His, mr is the slope of the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity vs absorbance of reference quinine sulphate, ns and nr are the refractive indices of the sample and reference, respectively, in the distilled water, which are assumed to be equal to that of water (1.33). The emission spectra for the sample were recorded at excitation wavelength of 320 nm, keeping slit width at 2 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of linker DNA (LDNA) with PEHA-GQD-His
ce:section.ce:para: LDNA was conjugated with PEHA-GQD-His by one-step carbodiimide reaction between the free carboxyl groups in LDNA and the free amino groups in PEHA-GQD-His [39]. In a typical procedure, the LDNA solution (1.0 μM, 50 μl) was dispersed in the TE buffer solution (1.0 ml) under ultrasound in a glass vial. Then, added the EDC solution (20 mg ml−1, 10 μl) and NHS solution (20 mg ml−1, 5 μl) to activate the free carboxyl groups in LDNA. After the mixed solution was incubated at room temperature for 90 min under shaking and free of light, added the PEHA-GQD-His in a PBS of pH 6.5 (0.2 mg ml-1, 250 μl). Followed by the incubation at room temperature for overnight under shaking and free of light. The resulting sample was ultrafiltrated using a Vivaspin concentrator (Sartorius, 10,000 MW) at 4000 g for 5 min at 4 °C to remove the non-conjugated LDNA. The obtained conjugates were ultrafiltrated with 1 × PBS twice. The collected LDNA-capped PEHA-GQD-His was kept at 4 °C prior to use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay for detection of miRNA
ce:section.ce:para: The amounts of LDNA-capped PEHA-GQD-His was added into the mixed solution of molecular beacon (MB) (40 μl, 100 nM), hairpin probe 1 (H1) (40 μl, 150 nM) and hairpin probe 2 (H2) (40 μl, 125 nM). Added 40 μl of the miRNA-141 standard solution with different concentration, followed by incubation at 37 °C for 90 min, addition of 40 μL of 0.1 μM freshly prepared hemin solution in the HEPES buffer, incubation at 37 °C for 30 min, and addition of 1 μl of 0.5 μM H2O2 in sequence. The resulting solution was placed for 5 min at room temperature and then its fluorescence spectrum or peak fluorescence intensity was measured using an excitation wavelength of 352 nm.

PEHA-GQD-His is elaborately designed as the optical probe to achieve ultrasensitive miRNA fluorescence biosensing based on dual recycling amplification strategy (shown in Fig. 1 ). In the design, citric acid mainly acts as the carbon source to make graphene sheets. Previous works have confirmed that citric acid molecules are easily converted into the graphene sheets composing of hexagonal sp2 carbon atoms by the intermolecular condensation and subsequent carbonization [40]. Both histidine and pentaethylenehexamine are mainly used to introduce special functional groups and structures to the edge of graphene sheets. Histidine contains the imidazole ring that offers strong proton exchange ability. The characteristic makes histidine in the horseradish peroxidase acts as the acid-based catalyst for improving the catalytic activity [41]. Thus, the main purpose of introducing histidine into GQD is to enhance the electrocatalytic activity of hemin towards H2O2. Different from histidine, pentaethylenehexamine includes two free amino groups and five imino groups in the one molecule. Thus the introduction of pentaethylenehexamine can greatly increase the charge density in graphene sheets due to strong electron donor effect of the amino and imino groups. This helps to improve the luminescent efficiency of GQDs and leads to an enhanced fluorescence emission. In addition, the introduction of pentaethylenehexamine also brings many free amino groups in the final PEHA-GQD-His. These free amino groups will be used for the covalent linkage between the EPHA-GQD-His and linker DNA sequence via the carbodiimide reaction.

The as-designed PEHA-GQD-His was synthesized by pyrolysis of the mixture of citric acid, pentaethylenehexamine and histidine. The resulting PEHA-GQD-His was characterized by TEM, high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), fast fourier transform pattern (FFTP), AFM and FTIR techniques. As shown in Fig. 2 , the PEHA-GQD-His is composed of 2D graphene sheets with the average size of 2.7 nm. Further, the results of HRTEM and FFTP analysis verify that the PEHA-GQD-His consists of crystalline hexagonal carbon network with the lattice spacing of 0.22 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphene crystal. The AFM analysis reveals that the average topographic height of PEHA-GQD-His is about 0.9 nm. The result confirms that the most of PEHA-GQD-His are made up of 1–2 graphene layers. There are several absorption peaks and bands on the FTIR spectrum. The band in the wavenumber range of 3300-3500 cm−1 is the characteristic IR absorptions of stretching vibration of OH and NH bonds. The band in the wavenumber range of 3000-2500 cm−1 is the IR absorption of C–H bond. The peak at 1700 cm−1 is corresponded to the stretching vibration of CO bond. The peak at 1500 cm-1 is due to the stretching vibration of C–O bond. The results of IR analysis demonstrate the existence of −OH, -NH2, −CH2, -C = O and –C–O in the PEHA-GQD-His.

Fig. 3 presents the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of PEHA-GQD-His. The excitation spectrum is mainly distributed in the wavelength range of 300–400 nm with the excitation peak at 352 nm. The excitation wavelength is much more than that of common organic compounds. This proves the fomation of graphene sheets with a large electronic delocalization range. Under the ultraviolet light irradiation of 352 nm, the PEHA-GQD-His gives a green fluorescence emission. The fluorescence emission spectrum exhibits a quasi symmetric shape with a fluorescence emission peak at 443 nm. To evaluate the luminescent efficiency, the fluorescence quantum yield of PEHA-GQD-His was measured and calculated by using quinine sulfate method [42]. The result indicates that the quantum yield of PEHA-GQD-His reaches to 90.2%. The value is much higher than that of the His-GQD prepared by pyrolysis of citric acid and histidine (32.5%) and other cabon-based luminescent materials reported in Table s1. We suggest that such a strong fluorescence emission is mainly attributed to the introduction of pentaethylenehexamine.

To study on the effect of PEHA-GQD-His on the electrocatalytic activity of hemin towards H2O2, the oxidation of tetramethylbenzidine with H2O2 by the hemin complex was used as a model reaction. In the reaction, the hemin complex acts as an artificial enzyme to catalyze H2O2 for producing the electron acceptor of O2 and results in a faster oxidation process of tetramethylbenzidine. Accompanied by the oxidation of tetramethylbenzidine with H2O2, the solution colour will gradually change from colourless into blue (shown in Fig. s1). The result indicates that the absorbance at 650 nm of the system is a reliable signal for our observation of electrocatalytic activity of the hemin complex towards H2O2 if the reaction time was fixed at 30 min. Namely, a bigger absorbance at 650 nm presents a higher electrocatalytic activity. The absorbance at 650 nm is close to zero after incubated for 30 min without the hemin complex. This is because the tetramethylbenzidine is difficult to be oxidized by H2O2 in the absence of electrocatalyst. The absorbance at 650 nm will largely increase after incubated for 30 min in the presence of hemin complex. The result verifies that the hemin complex can accelerate the oxidation of tetramethylbenzidine with H2O2, indicating high electrocatalytic activity. The absorbance will further increase in the presence of the hemin complex and PEHA-GQD-His. This proves that the PEHA-GQD-His can obviously improve electrocatalytic activity of the hemin complex towards H2O2. Fig. s1 also indicates that the PEHA-GQD-His can cause a much bigger increase in the absorbance compared with PEHA-GQD. The result confirms that the PEHA-GQD-His is superior to PEHA-GQD in improving catalytic activity of the hemin complex. The improved catalytic activity is mainly attributed to imidazole rings in PEHA-GQD-His.

The principle of as-proposed fluorescence biosensing nanoplatform for detection of miRNA is presented in Fig. 4 . The biosensing nanoplatform mainly includes DNA assembly on PEHA-GQD-His and DNAzyme-modulated fluorescence double quenching. To achieve highly efficient DNA assembly on the PEHA-GQD-His surface, MB, H1 and H2 are designed to trigger target cycle and MB cycle. In the design, MB base sequence contains two functional regions. One region is the toehold segment in the stem of MB for target miRNA recycling. Another region is the sequence segment for target miRNA recognition. Because the toehold segment and target recognition segment are locked in the stem of MB through the formation of duplex, MB can exist in a stable structure in the absence of target miRNA. H1 is composed of target displacement region, LDNA hybridization region, H2 toehold region and G-quadruplex sequence. In the presence of miRNA, the target miRNA specifically binds the complementary segment in MB and leads to the exposure of toehold region in the stem of MB. The exposed toehold region in MB hybridizes with the H1 to open hairpin H1, because the MB/H1 duplex with more base pairs is stronger than the MB/miRNA duplex. This results in the exposure of LDNA hybridization region, H2 toehold region and G-quadruplex sequence in the H1 and initates the DNA branch migration process to release miRNA for target recycling. The resulting H1-H2-LDNA nanostructure is assembled on the PEHA-GQD-His by immobilizing H1 on the LDNA-capped PEHA-GQD-His and the strand displacement of MB with H2. The released MB reuses to bind other target miRNAs to trigger new DNA nanoassembly. By continuous MB cycle and target cycle, many G-quadruplexes are presented on the PEHA-GQD-His surface.

To achieve highly efficient DNAzyme-modulated fluorescence double quenching, the presented G-quadruplexes in the PEHA-GQD-His surface bind hemin to produce the G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme. As the DNAzyme at 3′terminus of H1 is in close proximity to the PEHA-GQD-His, the photo excited electrons are effectively transferred from the PEHA-GQD-His into the G-quadruplex DNAzyme due to strong electron accepting ability of the DNAzyme. This leads to a significant fluorescence quenching of PEHA-GQD-His. On the other hand, the DNAzyme acts as an artificial enzyme to catalyze H2O2 for in situ generating quencher of O2, further quenching fluorescence of the PEHA-GQD-His. More importantly, our investigation has confirmed that the combination of DNAzyme with PEHA-GQD-His can greatly enhance the electrocatalytic activity of DNAzyme with H2O2. Thus, the DNAzyme-modulated fluorescence double quenching mechanism greatly improves the sensitivity of miRNA fluorescence biosensing nanoplatform.

The as-proposed fluorescence biosensing nanoplatform for detection of miRNA includes two key designs: DNA nanoassembly on the PEHA-GQD-His and DNAzyme-modulated double quenching. The DNA nanoassembly is achieved by target cycle and MB cycle. To evaluate the effect of target cycle and MB cycle, different biosensing schemes were designed and their fluorescence responses were measured. Fig. 5 indicates that the scheme of LDNA-capped PEHA-GQD-His, miRNA, H1, H2, hemin and H2O2 gives a small fluorescence quenching (curve b). Due to the lack of MB in the scheme, target cylce and MB cycle cann’t be effectively triggered (no cycle). Only weak non-specific hybridization exists in between PEHA-GQD-His, H1, H2 and hemin, thus producing a very low fluorescence queching signal (ΔF). However, the ΔF is obviously increased when H2 is replaced by MB. In the presence of MB but absence of H2, only target cycle can be achieved in the biosensing scheme (single cycle). Becasue the H1/MB can specifically bind with the LDNA-capped PEHA-GQD-His to form the complex via the target cycle. The formed H1/MB complex is close the proximity of PEHA-GQD-His surface and brings a more ΔF value. Among four schemes, the scheme of LDNA-capped PEHA-GQD-His, MB, miRNA, H1, H2, hemin and H2O2 gives the highest ΔF value. In the presence of miRNA, MB, H1 and H2, DNA nanoassembly on the PEHA-GQD-His surface can be efficiently achieved by miRNA cycle and MB cycle (double cycle) to form a large number of DNAzyme on the PEHA-GQD-His surface. These DNAzyme facilitates the DNAzyme-modulated double quenching process and brings a signficant fluorescence quenching. The above results verifies that the as-proposed fluorescence biosensing nanoplatform creates miRNA and MB double cycles. The design of double cycle plays important roles in improving the sensitivity of fluorescence miRNA biosensing.

To verify the existence of DNAzyme-modulated double quenching and test their effect on the fluorescence response towards miRNA-141, three means of fluorescence quenching are designed and their fluoresecence quenching behaviours were researched. Fig. 6 indicates that in the absence of hemin and H2O2 (miRNA mean) only DNA nanoassembly on the PEHA-GQD-His surface induces a fluorescence quenching process (curve a). The poto-excited electron transfer from the PEHA-GQD-His to H1-H2 causes a small fluorescence quenching signal (ΔF). When hemin was introduced into the system, G-quadruplexes on the PEHA-GQD-His surface binds hemin to form the G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme (hemin mean). Due to excellent electron acceptor ability, the formed G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme promotes the photo excited electron transfer from the PEHA-GQD-His to the DNAzyme, thus leading to a rapid fluorescence quenching process of PEHA-GQD-His (curve b). Its ΔF value of the hemin mean is more than 2.8-fold that of the miRNA mean. This verifies that the introduction of hemin brings a larger fluorescence quenching efficiency of PEHA-GQD-His. In the presence of hemin and H2O2 (hemin+H2O2 mean), the formed G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme can also catalyze H2O2 to in stiu generate the quencher of O2, further quenching the fluorescence of PEHA-GQD-His. The ΔF value of hemin+H2O2 mean is more than 2.6-fold that of the miRNA mean, indicating the existence of DNAzyme-modulated double quenching mechanism.

For the DNAzyme-modulated double quenching, the concentrations of hemim and H2O2 are important factors for influencing on the fluorescence quenching efficiency of PEHA-GQD-His. Herein, the fluorescence quenching behaviors of the reaction system with different concentration of hemin and H2O2 were tested. Fig.s2A presents fluorescence spectra of the reaction system with different concentration of hemin in the absence and presence of target miRNA-141. The hemin concentration not only affects on the background florescence intenstiy of the blank reaction system, but also affects the fluorescence quenching rate after added the target miRNA. In the absence of target miRNA, the background fluorescence intensity can remain stable when the hemin concentration is lower than 100 nM. However, the background fluorescence rapidly reduces with increasing hemin concentration when its concentration is more than 100 nM. This is attributed to non-specific hybridization between the PEHA-GQD-His and the excessive hemin molecules, due to non-specifc PET fluorescence quenching. In the presence of target miRNA, the fluorescence quenching rate will rapidly increase with increasing the hemin concentration when the concentration is lower than 100 nm. Continue to increase the hemin concentration, no significant change in fluorescence quenching rate can be observed in Fig. s2A. To further understand the effect of hemin concentration on the DNAzyme-modulated double quenching, the differences in peak florescence intensity without and with target miRNA (ΔF) are calculated at various concentration of hemin and the results are shown in Fig. s2B. Clearly, the hemin concentration of 100 nM gives the biggest ΔF value. To obtain a high sensitivity for target miRNA biosensing and reduce non-specific fluorescence quenching, 100 nM hemin was employed for the double fluorescence quenching.

The fluorescence spectra of reaction system with different concentration of H2O2 without and with target miRNA are presented in Fig. s3A. Without target miRNA no obvious change in the fluorescence intensity can be observed from Fig. s3A if the concentration of H2O2 is lower than 500 nM. Continue to increase the concentration of H2O2 results in a rapid reduce in fluorescence signal, owing to non-specific fluorescence quenching between the PEHA-GQD-His and the H2O2. With target miRNA the fluorescence quenching rate seriously depends on the concentration of H2O2. At H2O2 of 10 nM, the fluoresence quenching rate is very small. With increasing H2O2 the fluorescence quenching rate rapidly increases, verifying that the catalytic activity of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme with H2O2 plays important role in quenching fluorescence of PEHA-GQD-His. To further understand the effect of H2O2 on the miRNA biosensing strategy, the differences in peak florescence intensity without and with target miRNA (ΔF) are calculated from the data in Fig. s3A at various concentration of H2O2. Fig. s3B indicates that the ΔF value rapidly increases with increasing H2O2 if the concentration of H2O2 is lower than 500 nM. The ΔF value reaches the maximum at 500 nM of H2O2 and then rapidly decreaes with increasing H2O2. To obtain a high sensitivity and small non-specific fluorescence quenching, the H2O2 of 500 nM was employed for the fluorescence miRNA biosensing.

MiRNA-141 was used as a model analyte to examine the feasibility of as-proposed fluorescence biosensing nanoplatform. Fig. 7 A shows fluorescence spectra of the biosensing system containing different concentrations of miRNA-141. It can be seen from Fig. 7A that the fluorescence intensity will gradually reduce with increasing miRNA-141 concentration. This verifies that the fluorescence signal of sensing system depends on its miRNA-141 concentration. Further, the plot of peak fluorescence intensity (Fp) vs. logarithm of miRNA-141 concentration was shown in Fig. 7B. Fig. 7B shows that a good linear relationship existes in between the Fp and the logarithm of miRNA-141 when the miRNA concentration is in the range of 1.0 × 10−18-1.0 × 10-12 M. The correspinding linear correlation equation is: Fp=-25217 log[CmiRNA-141(M)] + 145,686 with the regression coefficient of 0.9998. The detection limit is low to 4.3 × 10-19 M based on the signal-to-noise ratio of 3. These data indicates that the sensitivity is obviously better than that of previously reported miRNA biosensors in Table 2 . In addition, the intra-assay imprecision of ten different detections at one assay and inter-assay imprecision at ten different batch PEHA-GQD-His for 1.0 × 10-14 miRNA-141 were tested to evaluate the reproducibility of as-proposed fluorescence biosensing nanoplatform. The intra-assay coefficient of variation (CV) is about 1.92% and the inter-assay CV is 3.17%. These data shows that the precision and reproducibility of the as-proposed fluorescence biosensing nanoplatform is acceptable.

The ultra high sensitivity of proposed fluorescence biosensing nanoplatform is attributed to the use of PEHA-GQD-His as the fluorescence probe unless the design of double cycles in the DNA nanoassembly and DNAzyme-modulated double quenching. On the one hand, the introduction of histidine into graphene sheets makes the combination of PEHA-GQD-His with the G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme greatly improves the catalytic activity towards H2O2. A better catalytic activity results in a bigger fluorescence quenching rate in the DNAzyme-modulated double quenching due to produce in stiu more O2 of quencher. On the other hand, the introduction of pentaethylenehexamine increases the charge density of graphene sheets and results in a stronger fluorescence emission. The strong fluorescence signal helps us to increase the signal-to-noise ratio of fluorescence detection and extend the range of fluorescence response towards miRNA. To quantitatively evaluate the effect of the introduction of histidine and pentaethylenehexamine on the sensitivity, two control fluorescence probes, pentaethylenehexamine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (PEHA-GQD) and hexamethylendiamine and histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (HDA-GQD-His), were made and used for the miRNA-141 fluorescence biosensing nanoplatform. Fig. s4A presents the fluorescence spectra of miRNA-141 fluorescence biosensing nanoplatform with PEHA-GQD, HAD-GQD-His and PEHA-GQD-His as the fluorescence probes in the absence and presence of miRNA. The PEHA-GQD has a similar structure with PEHA-GQD-His but lacks histidine groups. Its fluorescence quenching rate is about 11.04%, which is much lower than that caused by PEHA-GQD-His (43.75%). These data indicate that the introduction of histidine brings an increase of nearly four times in the fluorescence quenching rate. The result further verifies that the histidine groups in PEHA-GQD-His can remarkably improve the catalytic activity of the DNAzyme towards H2O2 and leads to a high fluorescence quenching rate. Further, Fig. s4B indicates that the fluorescence response of PEHA-GQD-His gives a more 5-fold fluorescence response towards miRNA-141 than that with PEHA-GQD. Both HDA-GQD-His and PEHA-GQD-His contain histidine groups, they exhibits a very close fluorescence quenching rate. However, the fluorescence response with the HDA-GQD-His towards miRNA-141 is much less than with PEHA-GQD-His, owing to its weak fluorescence emission. The above results demonstrate that the introduction of both histidine and pentaethylenehexamine plays important roles in improving the sensitivity of miRNA fluorescence biosensing nanoplatform.

Good selectivity is the fundamental guarantee for one analytical method to obtain a reliable result. For miRNA analysis, main interferences should be come from its family members because they often have several-base difference from each other. The present amplification strategies mostly achieved to the DNA chain migration by using differences in thermodynamic stability of the product formed after the hybridization. Thus, high sequence homology makes miRNA family members are difficulty to be distincted. To resolve the problem, target miRNA and MB double cycle is ingeniously designed in the as-proposed signal amplification strategy. Only specific hybridization of target miRNA with hairpin MB can trigger DNA assembly on the PEHA-GQD-His, causing a remarkable fluorescence quenching process. In the design, the above problem can be effectively avoided because target miRNA does not participate in the DNA assembly. Furthermore, the sequences of MB, H1 and H2, are reasonably designed to ensure that the DNA chain migration can be carried out in a stable and orderly manner. To test the selectivity of our miRNA detection protocol, the fluorescence response caused by different miRNA species such as miRNA-141, miRNA-21, miRNA-429, miRNA-155, single-base mismatched miRNA-141 (M-1) and two-base mismatched miRNA-141 (M-2), are investigated by using the same procedure. Fig. 8 shows that only miRNA-141 can cause a strong fluorescence quenching signal among these miRNA species, verifying that our fluorescence biosensing platform has a good selectivity to miRNA-141. Because MB contains a complementary segment that is specific for miRNA-141, it can specifically hybridize with miRNA-141 to trigger the target recycling and catalytic hairpin assembly on the PEHA-GQD-His surface. This results in a significant fluorescence quenching signal. However, other miRNA species can not effectively open hairpin MB due to their poor base complementarity. In the presence of miRNA-21, miRNA-429, M-1 and M-2, the DNA assembly on the PEHA-GQD-His and subsequent DNAzyme-modulated double quenching are difficult to be achieved. Thus, their existence only cause an extremely small non-specific fluoresence quenching and doesn’t interfere with the fluorescence detection of miRNA-141.

The proposed biosensor has been applied in fluorescence detection of miRNA-141 in real human serum samples. In addition, the spiked recovery experiments were also carried out by adding known concentrations of miRNA-141 stand into human serum samples. Table 3 shows that the analytical recoveries of miRNA-141 for these spiked human serum samples is in the range of 98.4–100.5%. The result verifies that the designed analytical method has good accuracy for fluorescence detection of miRNA.

Present study has demonstrated the design and synthesis of PEHA-GQD-His and its application in ultrasensitive fluorescence detection of microRNA based on target and molecular beacon double cycle amplification strategy. The result shows that the design of target and beacon double cycle creates highly specific and efficient DNA nanoassembly on the PEHA-GQD-His surface. This brings admirable selectivity and signal amplification capability for detection of miRNA. The DNAzyme-modulated double quenching achieves to high fluorescence quenching efficiency of the PEHA-GQD-His. The PEHA-GQD-His offers strong fluorescence emission and good ability to enhance the catalytic activity of G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzymes towards H2O2. These factors greatly improve the efficiency of DNAzyme-modulated double quenching and results in an obviously improved sensitivity. The as-proposed analytical method provides great advantage of sensitivity and selectivity for miRNA fluorescence biosensing. The study also provides a promising approach to synthesis of various graphene-based luminescent materials for different applications such as biosensors, bioimaging and electronic devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No.2018YFC1603001), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21576115), National First-class Discipline Program of Food Science and Technology (No. JUFSTR20180301), Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province Special Equipment Safety Supervision Inspection Institute (KJ(Y) 2017014) and MOE & SAFEA for the 111 Project (B13025).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.12.082.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
